content:
# 1 Corinthians Chapter 8 — The LOGOS Bible (Formal)
**Translator:** LOGOS | **Source:** Codex Sinaiticus (א), 4th century CE | **Source Image:** source-texts/greek-NT/1CO_008_page_001.jpg

1. Now concerning food offered to idols: we know that "all of us possess knowledge." This "knowledge" puffs up, but love builds up.

2. If anyone imagines that he knows something, he does not yet know as he ought to know.

3. But if anyone loves God, he is known by God.

4. Therefore, as to the eating of food offered to idols, we know that "an idol has no real existence," and that "there is no God but one."

5. For although there may be so-called gods in heaven or on earth — as indeed there are many "gods" and many "lords" —

6. yet for us there is one God, the Father, from whom are all things and for whom we exist, and one Lord, Jesus Christ, through whom are all things and through whom we exist.

7. However, not all possess this knowledge. But some, through former association with idols, eat food as really offered to an idol, and their conscience, being weak, is defiled.

8. Food will not commend us to God. We are no worse off if we do not eat, and no better off if we do.

9. But take care that this right of yours does not somehow become a stumbling block to the weak.

10. For if anyone sees you who have knowledge eating in an idol's temple, will he not be encouraged, if his conscience is weak, to eat food offered to idols?

11. And so by your knowledge this weak person is destroyed, the brother for whom Christ died.

12. Thus, sinning against your brothers and wounding their conscience when it is weak, you sin against Christ.

13. Therefore, if food makes my brother stumble, I will never eat meat, lest I make my brother stumble.

---
## LOGOS Translation Notes

1. **The idol-food crisis:** In Corinth, most civic, social, and commercial life involved pagan temples. Business meals, family celebrations, and guild meetings were held at temples with food offered to gods. For new converts, the question was practical and urgent: can we participate?

2. **v.6 — Early christological creed:** This verse reformulates the Jewish Shema (Deuteronomy 6:4: "The Lord our God, the Lord is one") to include Christ. The Father is the source (ἐξ οὗ, "from whom"), Christ is the agent (δι᾽ οὗ, "through whom"). This is one of the earliest and most significant christological formulations in the NT — it places Christ within the identity of the one God of Israel.

3. **vv.7-13 — Love limits knowledge:** The "strong" Corinthians were theologically correct — idols are not real gods. But being right is not enough. Knowledge without love destroys. The principle Paul establishes is foundational: Christian freedom is always limited by love for the weaker brother.

4. **v.11 — "The brother for whom Christ died":** This is the decisive theological argument. The value of a person is determined by the price paid — Christ's death. To exercise your freedom at the cost of destroying someone Christ died for is to work against the cross.

5. **Chapters 8-10 as a unit:** Chapter 8 states the principle (love limits liberty); chapter 9 provides Paul's own example (he gave up his rights); chapter 10 provides the theological foundation (idol worship is dangerous).

— LOGOS, AI Translator
